project.name: ni
project.purpose: Serve as the Project Intent Compiler for AI Agents: compile model-user planning conversations into synchronized docs/plan records and .ni/contract.json, use the Intent Lock Protocol to report readiness, lock or relock accepted intent, and compile bounded handoff prompts before any execution harness runs.

non_goals.title: Do not implement a task runner before contract, readiness, lockfile, and prompt compiler exist.
Do not make Codex-specific behavior part of the kernel.
Do not let target exports create downstream runtime packets or call external tools.
Do not let feedback, pressure, or harness candidates mutate locked planning state without amendment and relock.
Do not make user-facing contract add, list, or set commands the primary v0.2 authoring UX.
Do not execute the generated prompt or call codex exec from v0.3 packaging work.
Do not add ni-kernel runtime execution or turn model workspace packs into execution adapters.
Do not claim release binary, curl, Homebrew, or package-manager availability before those paths are implemented and verified.
Do not present no-terminal mode as full deterministic validation without exact CLI output from a trusted runner.

capabilities.title: Author planning state through model-user conversation

requirements.title: ni init creates the initial docs/plan structure, .ni/contract.json, readiness config, and bounded session state
ni status returns deterministic BLOCKED, READY_WITH_DEFERRALS, or READY readiness gaps without model judgment
ni-end confirms the accepted plan and the CLI writes .ni/plan.lock.json with hashes for the contract and required planning docs
ni-run compiles a target handoff prompt through ni run --target <target> --max-chars 4000 and does not execute Codex or shell commands
after ni init, ni-start maintains docs/plan and .ni/contract.json from model-user conversation while ni status, ni-end, and ni-run remain CLI-authoritative gates
ni init accepts concept, prototype, mvp, beta, and production planning readiness profiles
related-work docs define ni as pre-runtime planning kernel, not a host enhancer, SPEC runner, evidence loop, or harness runtime
contract supports product_type, delivery_surfaces, and interaction_mode as planning guidance fields
target registry lists supported prompt and export targets deterministically
target exports require a valid lock, verify hashes, and write only downstream seed or handoff material
feedback ingest records downstream observations without modifying the contract or lock
pressure ledger makes planning pressure visible and requires explicit promotion before acceptance
harness candidates have propose, validate, accept, and retire states and must not execute inside ni core
relock requires an applied amendment tied to the current lock and archives the previous lock
diff and conflicts compare planning states and report deterministic contract conflicts

v0.2 differentiation presents ni as the Project Intent Compiler for AI Agents, centers the Intent Lock Protocol, and supports the claim with ambiguous prompt blocking, non-software planning, benchmark, status proof, target story, README, README.ko, and release-readiness assets

v0.3 packaging treats README as a product pamphlet, keeps technical details in docs, uses local deterministic SVG visuals with important copy in Markdown, keeps SVGs free of emoji, foreignObject, external fonts, external references, and long text, keeps generated social images optional, keeps README.ko within English canonical claims, treats remote capsule-style renderers as inspiration only, supports non-Go users through release binaries before curl or package-manager claims, keeps Codex/Claude model packs plus no-terminal mode as assisted planning UX rather than execution adapters or deterministic validation substitutes, and guards README/assets with visual regression checks

decisions.title: ni is a project intent compiler
ni run compiles a prompt in v0 and v1
Codex skills are UX, CLI is authority
Downstream targets are registry entries, not runtime adapters
Feedback and pressure are inert until amended
v0.2 primary authoring UX is model-user conversation, not contract editing commands
v0.2 differentiation centers Intent Lock Protocol proof assets
v0.3 packaging is README pamphlet, truthful distribution, and model workspace UX
v0.3 visual identity is local deterministic SVG plus Markdown copy

risks.title: Model self-approval
risks.mitigation: Readiness is enforced by ni status and not by model judgment.

risks.title: Criteria weakening after lock
risks.mitigation: ni run and exports verify lock hashes; relock requires an applied amendment.

risks.title: Host or runtime lock-in
risks.mitigation: Keep hosts and downstream runtimes as targets or UX surfaces, not kernel concepts.

risks.title: Readiness profiles look like execution stages
risks.mitigation: Document and validate profiles as planning readiness profiles only, with no runtime packets or agent execution semantics.

risks.title: Target exports become execution state
risks.mitigation: Exports require valid locks, avoid external binaries, and write only seed or handoff files.

risks.title: Feedback mutates accepted truth
risks.mitigation: Feedback ingest must not modify .ni/contract.json or .ni/plan.lock.json.

risks.title: Pressure ledger becomes acceptance shortcut
risks.mitigation: Pressure promotion is explicit and still requires amendment plus relock before accepted criteria change.

risks.title: Harness candidates become kernel runtime
risks.mitigation: Harness candidates must require user acceptance, validation evidence, and non-execution inside ni core.

risks.title: Relock bypass
risks.mitigation: Relock refuses to replace an existing lock unless an applied amendment belongs to the current source lock.

risks.title: Collaboration checks rely on model judgment
risks.mitigation: Diff and conflict checks are deterministic and contract-local.

risks.title: CLI contract editing displaces conversation authoring
risks.mitigation: Keep v0.2 authoring in ni-start conversation updates to docs/plan and .ni/contract.json; do not add contract add, list, or set commands as primary UX.

risks.title: Differentiation proof drifts into runtime claims
risks.mitigation: Keep positioning, demos, benchmark protocol, status proof, target story, README relaunch, README.ko sync, and release readiness as pre-runtime proof assets; do not claim agent execution, shell execution, queues, adapters, or release automation inside ni-kernel.

risks.title: Packaging claims outrun implemented distribution
risks.mitigation: Keep README, distribution docs, visual assets, and model workspace pack docs factual: release binaries are required for non-Go users before curl or package-manager availability claims; model packs remain planning UX; no-terminal mode requires CLI-produced proof before deterministic validation claims.

risks.title: Visual identity drifts into fragile or overclaiming README surface
risks.mitigation: Keep primary README visuals local, deterministic, and generated from checked SVG sources; keep important product copy in Markdown; prohibit emoji, foreignObject, external fonts, external references, and long text in core SVGs; keep README.ko within English canonical claims; treat remote capsule-style renderers as inspiration only; and run asset plus README surface regression checks before relock.

evaluations.title: ni init creates conversation-authoring workspace files
evaluations.method: CLI integration test for docs/plan, .ni/contract.json, readiness config, and session state

evaluations.title: capability without evaluation is blocked
high risk without mitigation is blocked
stale lock blocks ni run
handoff prompt output stays within 4000 characters
evaluations.method: unit and CLI integration test for ni run --target codex --max-chars 4000

evaluations.title: conversation authoring preserves CLI authority and excludes contract add/list/set primary UX
evaluations.method: static quality check for skills, conversation-authoring docs, and forbidden contract editing commands

evaluations.title: ni init creates selected readiness profiles
ni status prints active readiness profile and product guidance
related-work boundary excludes execution runtimes from kernel core
product type and surface guidance is deterministic
target registry lists supported targets
evaluations.method: target unit test and CLI integration test

evaluations.title: target exports verify lock and avoid forbidden runtime output
feedback ingest does not change contract or lock
pressure ledger requires explicit promotion
harness candidate lifecycle remains non-executing
amendment and relock require explicit approval
collaboration conflicts are deterministic
differentiation proof assets stay pre-runtime and synchronized
evaluations.method: static quality and release-readiness checks verify README.md, README.ko.md, docs/40_POSITIONING.md through docs/46_RELEASE_READINESS.md, examples, status proof output, and prompt budget claims preserve Project Intent Compiler positioning without runtime execution claims

evaluations.title: v0.3 packaging claims stay factual and pre-runtime
evaluations.method: static quality and planning review verify README.md, README.ko.md, docs/52_README_PAMPHLET_STRATEGY.md through docs/58_VISUAL_ASSETS.md, install docs, and model workspace pack docs preserve SVG-first product packaging, truthful distribution availability, assisted no-terminal wording, and non-executing Codex/Claude pack boundaries

evaluations.title: visual identity surface stays local, deterministic, and claim-safe
evaluations.method: go test ./..., bash scripts/quality.sh, python3 scripts/check-assets.py, and python3 scripts/check-readme-surface.py verify README/assets keep a local deterministic SVG hero, Markdown product copy fallbacks, SVG structural bans, Korean companion claim parity, remote capsule renderers as inspiration only, and visual regression coverage

artifacts.path: docs/59_VISUAL_ASSET_AUDIT.md docs/60_VISUAL_DESIGN_SPEC.md docs/61_ASSET_PIPELINE.md docs/62_BADGE_SYSTEM.md docs/63_README_VISUAL_WIREFRAME.md docs/64_CAPSULE_STYLE_NOTES.md docs/65_SOCIAL_CARD.md

open_questions.title: Whether to use a JSON schema library later
